rankings.writeup: Team Aron takes the top spot after delivering the statement win of the season — a 70-51 demolition of previously unbeaten Nathan. Three players scored 16 or more: Josh Sun (21 pts, 7 ast), Jonathan Chung (16 pts, 10 reb), and Jon Han (16 pts, 9 reb). That kind of balanced attack is hard to gameplan against. Sun continues to be the league's most versatile player, now averaging 18.0 PPG and 3.3 APG while playing lockdown defense (7 steals in 3 games). The league's best point differential (+8.3) tells you this isn't a fluke — Aron wins by suffocation on defense and balance on offense.

Still the best record in the league, but Week 3 exposed Team Nathan's Achilles heel: depth. Without Austin Im and Ryan Choi — their second and third-leading scorers — Nathan Paik put the team on his back with 33 points, but on a brutal 9-for-38 from the field (8-for-26 from three). When the supporting cast is there, this team is arguably the best in the OBA. When it's not, it becomes the Nathan Paik show on high volume, low efficiency. The good news? Paik is still averaging 28.0 PPG and 10.0 RPG, the best stat line in the league. If the full squad returns, they're still the team to beat.

Christian Kim put up the most dominant stat line of the season — 22 points and 20 rebounds in the 56-42 win over PJo. Twenty boards. That's not a typo. Kim is now averaging 21.0 PPG and a league-best 15.0 RPG, establishing himself as the most dominant big man in the OBA. Sean Lee remains a steady hand at 21.3 PPG, but the real question is whether the supporting cast can step up in bigger games. The 21-point loss to Nathan in Week 2 still looms, and this team needs a third scoring option to emerge if it wants to contend.

Team Matt gets off the schneid with a 49-41 win over PJo, and Matt Paik led the way with a well-rounded 22-point, 10-rebound, 3-block stat line. Chris Hong broke out with 12 points after a quiet debut, and the defense held PJo to just 41 points — the second-lowest team total this season. Only two games in the books makes it tough to rank them higher, but the pieces are there: Kanghee Lee's rebounding (7.5 RPG), David Kim's efficiency, and Matt Paik's ability to get to the free throw line (17-for-20 FT on the season). More data needed, but the arrow is pointing up.

It's been a rough start for Team PJo, who remain winless after dropping both games in Week 3. The 41-49 loss to Matt and 42-56 loss to Sean bring them to 0-4 with a league-worst -9.3 point differential. The talent is there on paper — Wonjin Jang is averaging a double-double (16.7 PPG, 10.3 RPG) and PJo himself puts up 11.8 PPG and 11.0 RPG — but the supporting cast has been inconsistent. Joseph Yoo (13.5 PPG in Week 1) hasn't played since, and nobody outside the top two has been a reliable scorer. At 0-4, every game is now a must-win if they want to stay in the playoff conversation.
